block1.title: Peeking & Angle Mechanics
block1.subtitle: Core knowledge for every duel — how fights are opened, won, and lost.

items.name: Angle Advantage Rule
items.source: Zasko — "3 Simple Ways To Improve Your Crosshair Placement"
items.why: Whoever is further from the corner gets revealed first — their body enters the opponent's screen before they can see the opponent's body. This is geometry, not aim. Understanding this means you can win duels before they start by simply adjusting your distance from the wall.
items.preview: Further from wall = spots enemy first. Closer to wall = disadvantage — you're revealed before you can see them. Check who has the angle advantage before committing. If you have advantage: trace carefully. If you're disadvantaged: pre-aim the expected spot or wide-swing to deny them the easy first shot.

items.name: Trace vs. Pre-Aim Decision
items.source: Zasko — "How (And When) To ACTUALLY Trace Angles"
items.why: Using the wrong technique for your angle is one of the most common mechanical errors. Tracing at a disadvantage angle means your crosshair lags behind and the enemy gets a free first shot. Pre-aiming when you have advantage means you hold a static position that the enemy can learn and pre-fire. The decision must be context-driven, not habitual.
items.preview: Trace = sliding crosshair along wall as you peek (use when you have angle advantage — further from wall). Pre-aim = lock crosshair on the spot before you peek (use when disadvantaged — enemy will appear before you fully see them). Speed during trace controls depth: fast peek = crosshair stays tight to wall; slow peek = crosshair drifts wider.

items.name: Acoustic-Based Hold Width
items.source: Zasko — "Stop GUESSING How Wide You Should Hold"
items.why: Running footsteps reveal two facts: the enemy is approaching (not lurking) and they're moving at full sprint speed. A sprinting enemy's first shot is inaccurate, so holding wide is correct — you can fire while they're still decelerating. Silence means a lurker who's already stopped and pre-aimed tight — holding wide against them gives them your entire body to shoot at while you search for a pixel.
items.preview: Running footsteps = hold WIDE (crosshair at far edge of corner). Enemy is sprinting, their first shot will miss. Silence / shift-walking = hold TIGHT (crosshair near wall edge). Enemy is pre-aimed and waiting. Let audio dictate your crosshair distance before the fight even starts.

items.name: Peeker's Advantage — How It Actually Works
items.source: n0ted — "How RADIANT's Abuse Peekers Advantage" / w0rthyTV
items.why: Peeker's advantage is a real, documented ~100ms timing gap — not a myth. When you move out to peek, the server registers your new position before the defender's client fully renders you and can react. This means you see the defender and start shooting before they even see you on their screen. Reaction time is NOT in the equation — both players react at the same speed. The advantage is purely who sees who first. As a peeker, hesitating removes this advantage entirely — a half-committed peek gives defenders time to catch up.
items.preview: You see the defender ~100ms before they see you. Use it: commit fully to peeks — no slow creeping, no half-swings. As a defender: never hold the same static spot after being peeked — reposition constantly so peeker must track a moving target, not pre-aim a known pixel.

items.name: Poisoned Angles (Anti-Repeeking)
items.source: Zasko — "How Canezerra Can BREAK The Rules"
items.why: The moment you show yourself at an angle, the enemy locks their crosshair at your exact pixel. Re-peeking there hands them a pre-aimed first shot — you lose peeker's advantage and they gain it. The angle is "poisoned" and stays poisoned until you reset with utility or approach from completely different geometry.
items.preview: Once you've peeked an angle, the enemy's crosshair is already there. Never re-peek the same spot at the same elevation. Your options: flash underneath first, throw a cloud/smoke, crouch to break their height expectation, or relocate entirely. "Don't do it dry."

items.name: Straight-Line Only Peeking (No Diagonal)
items.source: w0rthyTV — "You Move Like A Bot" / Knight Protocol
items.why: Adding W on top of a lateral strafe (pressing W+A or W+D) stacks forward velocity on top of your sideways velocity — higher total speed means significantly worse first-shot accuracy. Single-key lateral movement (A or D only) keeps velocity lower, preserving accurate first shots at the critical moment you're exposed. When corner geometry forces a forward-then-sideways approach, the Knight Protocol gives you straight-line movement at each phase without combining velocities.
items.preview: Use A or D only when peeking — never W+A or W+D together. The combined velocity ruins your first shot. Tight corner forcing a diagonal? Use the Knight Protocol: step forward (W only) to create separation, then strafe laterally (A or D only). Each phase is pure straight-line movement.

items.name: Never Repeat an Angle Without Utility
items.source: w0rthyTV — "Why You Get 1 Tapped Holding Angles"
items.why: After you've been seen at an angle, the enemy pre-aims your exact pixel and will fire the moment you appear again. No amount of aim overcomes being pre-fired at a known location. Utility (flash, cloud, smoke) physically disrupts the visual information they're relying on. Without it, you are giving them a guaranteed free first shot.
items.preview: After taking a fight on an angle, the enemy knows your exact pixel. Never return to the same spot without: flashing to break their pre-aim, throwing a cloud or smoke, or approaching from a completely different angle. Returning dry = giving them a free kill.

items.name: On-Angle vs. Off-Angle
items.source: w0rthyTV — UFOs Framework / "How To Win ALL Your Gunfights With ANGLES"
items.why: On-angles are locations where enemies have trained crosshair placement from repetition — at Ascendant+, pre-fires on common spots are muscle memory. Off-angles remove this advantage entirely, converting the prepared holder into a reactor who has to find you first. The value compounds late in rounds: with 15 seconds left, rushing enemies skip off-angle checks entirely.
items.preview: On-angles (common spots): enemies pre-place crosshairs there — expect pre-fires. Fight by peeking in and out constantly to bait shots. Off-angles (unexpected spots): enemies have no crosshair placement — you have full peeker's advantage plus surprise. Use off-angles more as the round timer drops.

items.name: Peeking Mechanics Are a Combo, Not Improvisation
items.source: Zasko — "you're killing your own aim" / "How to ACTUALLY angle hold properly"
items.why: Improvised peeking has high variance. Pre-programmed if-then combos (slice → condition → wide swing) remove decision latency from the equation — the combo fires automatically under pressure because it was installed through deliberate practice, not assembled in real-time. Zasko compares it to Muay Thai: a trained fighter doesn't think about combinations mid-fight.
items.preview: 4 tools: Slice (info + condition), Wide Swing (kill after conditioning), Angle Hold (wait for them), Jiggle/Jump peek (bait shots). Rules: never open with a wide swing — you have no info. Always slice first to gather information. Wide swing only after conditioning the enemy crosshair. Treat combos like Muay Thai: pre-programmed if-then sequences.

items.name: Left/Right Angle Symmetry (CS:GO Myth)
items.source: n0ted — "Radiant University EP.4 noted's peeking guide"
items.why: In CS:GO, the camera is offset from the model center, creating a documented right-eye peek advantage. Valorant does NOT have this — the camera is centered, and the viewmodel toggle is purely cosmetic. Left-side angles are not mechanically harder; if they feel harder, it is crosshair placement or habit. Shoulder-bait remains useful on any angle not because of a camera offset, but because it draws the enemy's first shot before your full body is exposed.
items.preview: Valorant has NO left-side penalty — camera is centered (viewmodel toggle is cosmetic only). CS:GO's right-eye advantage does NOT apply here. Left-side difficulty = crosshair placement habit, not engine mechanics. Shoulder-bait is still valuable: draw their first shot with a shoulder peek, then wide-swing while they reset.

items.name: Knight Protocol (L-Shape Peeking)
items.source: w0rthyTV — "You Move Like A Bot (Movement Guide)"
items.why: Tight corners tempt diagonal movement. Pressing W+A or W+D stacks forward velocity on top of your lateral strafe — higher total speed means worse first-shot accuracy. The Knight move gives you the same angle exposure with straight-line movement at each phase, keeping velocity lower during the critical peek.
items.preview: Tight wall preventing a straight-line peek? Step forward first (W only) to create separation, then strafe laterally (A or D only) — the chess knight's L-shape. Two straight-line phases instead of one diagonal motion. Each phase is lower-velocity and more accurate. Practice on any tight corner until it becomes automatic.

items.name: Overstaying Your Welcome
items.source: w0rthyTV — "Why You CANT Hold Angles In VALORANT"
items.why: Each additional second in a compromised position increases the probability of a coordinated push or utility flush. Your life is worth two points: the body and the position it controls. Dying while overstaying costs both. The moment you've extracted value (a kill, information, or a forced rotate), the math says leave — staying drops your value to near-zero while the risk stays at 100%.
items.preview: After a kill or successful utility use, leave immediately. Signs you're overstaying: spent utility, multiple enemies now aware, team committed elsewhere. Your life = 2 points (body + position). Every extra second drops your escape probability. Extract the value, then go.

block2.title: Movement & Mechanics Physics
block2.subtitle: What actually happens to your character's accuracy and speed — the science under the mechanics.

items.name: Movement Speed Curve
items.source: n0ted — "Valorant Movement MASTERCLASS" / "Counter-Strafe Matters"
items.why: Valorant's accuracy ties directly to movement speed — it's not binary (moving vs. stopped). As you decelerate, bullet spread shrinks and full accuracy returns once velocity drops below roughly 30% of max speed. You don't need to reach zero — just slow enough. Counter-strafing (pressing the opposite key) returns velocity to zero faster than releasing alone, but Valorant decelerates quickly regardless, so the difference is only a few milliseconds.
items.preview: Accuracy returns before a full stop — once you're below ~30% max speed. Shoot during deceleration, not just at full stop. Counter-strafing saves a few milliseconds vs. just releasing the key. Enable the shooting error graph in the range: yellow lines = accurate, blue lines = still moving too fast. Train by feel, not by memorized millisecond numbers.

items.name: Shadow Stepping (Tap-W Movement)
items.source: w0rthyTV — "Movement & Peeking Mastery"
items.why: Brief 1-2 step bursts of movement let you reposition between cover without triggering full footstep audio — you stay below the audio threshold. The reliably silent options are shift-walking and standing still. Shadow steps also benefit accuracy: each short burst returns to near-zero velocity at the direction change, giving you accurate shots without a full stop.
items.preview: For guaranteed silence: shift-walk. For quick silent repositioning: tap W (1-2 steps only, then stop). Never full-sprint near enemies unless you want them to hear you. Shadow steps are especially useful for repositioning off an angle you've already been seen at, or adjusting your cover position mid-round.

items.name: Crosshair Acceptance Rule
items.source: Zasko — "You're Making Gunfights WAY Harder Than They Should Be"
items.why: Mid-motion crosshair correction creates a curved, chaotic path that is nearly impossible to control precisely. Stopping completely first converts the correction into a linear micro-flick — a fundamentally simpler movement your fine motor system can execute with high precision. The Goalie Stance analogy: a still goalkeeper can dive in any direction; one already leaning can't redirect.
items.preview: During a peek, if your crosshair lands slightly off — do NOT fix it while still moving. Stop completely first. Accept where it landed. Execute one clean micro-flick after full stop. Moving the mouse while strafing creates a messy curved path. Stop → one linear flick = clean.

items.name: Vandal vs. Phantom Mechanics
items.source: Fundamenty / n0ted
items.why: The Vandal and Phantom have identical movement-accuracy mechanics. Their real differences: Phantom has tighter first-shot accuracy (spread 0.20 vs. 0.25 for Vandal — 20% more forgiving) and a faster fire rate (11 rps vs. 9.75 rps). Vandal deals flat 160 headshot damage at all ranges; Phantom has damage falloff at distance. The Vandal's lower first-shot tolerance makes timing errors immediately visible, which is why it feels punishing.
items.preview: Vandal and Phantom = same movement mechanics, different margin for error. Phantom: faster fire rate (11 vs 9.75 rps), more forgiving first shot (0.20 vs 0.25 spread), damage drops off at range. Vandal: flat 160 headshot damage everywhere, demands cleaner timing. Train them in separate DM sessions — weapon-switching builds habit confusion.

items.name: Don't Lift Your Fingers
items.source: n0ted — "How to Unf*ck Your Aim Part II"
items.why: Physically lifting a finger to press the opposite direction key adds transit time — the finger must travel up, across, and down. This window is long enough to cost you accuracy timing in a fast strafe sequence. Keeping all four movement fingers resting on their keys at all times means direction changes happen through simultaneous key engagement with zero travel time.
items.preview: Never lift your hand when changing direction (A→D). Correct: A held → D immediately engaged (simultaneously), no physical lift. Fingers rest on all movement keys at all times. Lifting feels natural but adds 30–60ms of dead time — that's the window you need to shoot accurately.

items.name: Crouch — When to Use It (and When Not To)
items.source: Zasko — "He Already Had INSANE Mechs"
items.why: Crouching as an accuracy crutch is punished at higher ranks because enemies have trained the ability to instantly re-aim at crouched head level. Below Immortal, random crouch mid-burst occasionally confuses spray-and-pray opponents. Above Immortal, the enemy's crosshair follows you down faster than you crouch. Use crouch tactically to break conditioning — not as your default accuracy fix.
items.preview: Crouch tap peek: light tap only (don't hold). Exploits enemy expectation of full-crouch spray — gives a split-second head advantage. Heavy crouch commit: only in isolated fights at a known position with no shoulder threats. Rule of thumb: below Immortal, avoid crouching (body shot risk). Immortal 2+: exploit via taps and selective heavies only.

items.name: Aim Is 90% Movement
items.source: w0rthyTV — MASS Protocol analysis of Florescent
items.why: Pro-level Valorant is movement-driven: the crosshair arrives on the target via WASD strafe positioning, not mouse flicking. Analysis of Florescent's play shows almost no flicking — the crosshair is pre-positioned through movement and the mouse only makes a small final correction. WASD keys can reposition your crosshair faster and more predictably than a flick, without the aim error that rushing a flick introduces. Flicking is compensating for failed positioning.
items.preview: Your movement keys do 90% of the aiming work. Use WASD to strafe your crosshair into position, mouse for the final small correction. The goal: by the time you stop moving, your crosshair is already on the head. If you're constantly flicking, your positioning is off — fix the positioning, not the flick.

items.name: Hyper Tap (Hall Effect Keyboards)
items.source: n0ted — "Hyper Tap is ADDICTING"
items.why: Traditional mechanical switches have 1.2–2.0mm pretravel before actuation. Hall Effect / magnetic switches (Wooting 60HE, Razer Analog) can actuate at 0.1mm minimum. The competitive advantage is not raw press speed — it's the rapid-trigger feature, which resets actuation in 0.1mm increments, enabling faster direction-change resets in counter-strafe sequences. This matters most at high strafe frequencies found in Immortal+ play.
items.preview: Hall Effect keyboards (e.g., Wooting 60HE): minimum actuation 0.1mm, total click latency ~1.1ms. Real advantage: rapid trigger resets in 0.1mm → faster A/D direction-change resets for counter-strafing. Standard keyboards introduce lag in rapid-direction-change sequences. Worth considering at Immortal+ where strafe frequency is high enough for this to matter.

block3.title: Mental Frameworks & Performance
block3.subtitle: The cognitive and psychological layer — what your mind does in fights and between them.

items.name: Green / Pink Mindset
items.source: Zasko — "This Subtle Mistake WASTES So Much Time"
items.why: Motor skill execution uses different neural pathways than analytical thinking. Bringing conscious analysis into a gunfight literally activates the wrong part of your brain — the prefrontal cortex is slower and less efficient for motor execution than your trained automatic system. Green/Pink is not about attitude; it's about routing your neural processing to the correct system for the task.
items.preview: Green = performance mode: flow state, intuition, trust mechanics. For warmup, comp, and DM. Pink = learning mode: analytical, deliberate, critical. For drills, VOD review, and end-of-session training only. Never go Pink during ranked. Mechanics = motor skill — cannot improve by thinking mid-gunfight. Game sense = CAN improve by thinking during comp.

items.name: Text Message Anticipation
items.source: Zasko — "You'll Never RUSH Your Shots Again"
items.why: Surprise is the largest driver of panic and missed shots. When an enemy appears unexpectedly, your startle reflex fires before your trained motor response — the reflex wins every time. Pre-visualizing scenarios before they happen removes surprise entirely. When the event occurs, your brain processes it as "this is what I predicted" rather than "unexpected threat," bypassing the startle response.
items.preview: Before taking space, mentally preview what you'll see. "Mid might push through smoke as I walk up. B player might be peeking long." When the enemy appears — it's a predicted event, not a surprise. No panic, no reflex miss. After each death: collect the scenario, prepare for it next round. Radiant game sense is just more predictions sent per minute.

items.name: Inner Game / Self 1 vs. Self 2
items.source: Zasko — "You'll Never Rank Up Until You Fix This" (Inner Game of Tennis)
items.why: The Inner Game of Tennis framework identifies that critical self-talk (Self 1) actively degrades performance by pulling conscious attention into motor execution that runs better automatically. Tilt is not just an emotion — it is a specific interference pattern where Self 1 hijacks the motor execution system. The fix is non-judgmental observation, which prevents interference without suppressing it.
items.preview: Self 1 (critic/thinker) causes tilt when dominant. Self 2 (doer/body) is what executes perfectly. Goal in ranked: give all control to Self 2. Tilt = Self 1 hijacking Self 2. Fix: observe without judgment — just notice what happens. Missing a shot = feedback, not failure. Replace "I'm so bad" with "interesting, what happened there?"

items.name: 0% Excuses / 100% Responsibility
items.source: w0rthyTV — "AIM is NOT Your Issue, THIS is"
items.why: Blaming creates a fixed attribution (it was their fault) that closes the loop without generating a solution. Taking responsibility creates an open attribution (what could I do differently?) that forces problem-solving. Over 100+ games, this difference compounds into very different rank trajectories — one gets stuck, one climbs.
items.preview: "If we blame, we stay the same." When a teammate does something wrong: ask "How was I responsible for that outcome?" When you die: ask "What could I have done differently?" Tilt is rooted in victimhood — it's looking for something to blame. Responsibility = solutions-based thinking = growth.

items.name: Investing RR (Reframe Losses)
items.source: w0rthyTV — coaching content
items.why: The emotional pain of losing RR triggers the same neural response as a financial loss — loss aversion. This creates tilt through a neurological mechanism, not a character weakness. Reframing a loss as "investing RR into information about my current weaknesses" changes the emotional tag from negative to neutral-to-positive. Every loss contains information that a win conceals.
items.preview: Reframe losses from "losing RR" to "investing RR." Every loss makes mistakes visible that wins hide. You always get something from a loss: where your mechanics broke down, where your positioning failed, what your current ceiling is. Eventual success built on understood losses is more durable than success built on blind wins.

items.name: Mental Diet (Copium Analogy)
items.source: w0rthyTV — "How MENTAL Affects your AIM"
items.why: Blame and negative self-talk are habitual thought patterns — every time you complete the blame loop, you strengthen the neural pathway for it. It becomes easier to blame next time. This compounds over hundreds of games until blame is the automatic first response to any death. Like diet: each individual instance seems harmless, but the cumulative effect controls your performance ceiling.
items.preview: What you feed your mental compounds. Blaming teammates = eating bad every day — day 1 feels fine, day 30 it's a habit that's controlling your play. High arousal/fear states degrade fine motor performance (grip tension, over-flicking). After every death: redirect energy to strategy and next-round planning, not blame. The habit of blame is more limiting than your aim.

items.name: Pele Protocol (Pre-Round Visualization)
items.source: Fundamenty — "Pele Protocol"
items.why: When you mentally rehearse an action, your brain fires nearly the same motor circuits as when you physically perform it. This is not wishful thinking — it is actual neural preparation. Elite athletes consistently use pre-event visualization, and FPS players benefit the same way: mentally seeing the peek, the stop, the headshot before it happens primes your motor circuits so execution feels like recall rather than improvisation. Surprise is eliminated. Your first fight becomes a predicted event.
items.preview: During buy phase: close your eyes and mentally preview the first fight you'll take. Visualize the exact angle, the two steps, the stop, the headshot. See it as a movie — complete and successful. When the real fight happens, your body recognizes it as a predicted event and executes without panic. 20–30 seconds per round. Build the habit.

items.name: Flow State Triggers
items.source: Zasko — "You'll Never Rank Up Until You Fix This"
items.why: Flow state is defined by four conditions: clear goal, immediate feedback, challenge at skill level, and loss of self-consciousness. All four are engineerable. The reset ritual between rounds directly addresses self-consciousness (breathe, loosen, release). Choosing one cue before queue creates the clear goal. Flow is not luck — it's a repeatable state you build conditions for.
items.preview: Flow emerges when: trusting mechanics (not analyzing them), clear single goal, immediate feedback, challenge slightly above skill level. Between rounds: mute game sound, take one deep breath, loosen shoulders, release mouse grip tension. The reset ritual IS the flow trigger. Don't skip it even in casual games.

items.name: Pre-Round Fight Planning
items.source: OD26 — "Why Everyone Hates Reyna Players"
items.why: Entering a round without a specific fight intention means your first contact is improvised and reactionary. Decision latency in the moment costs 200–400ms that you can't recover once the fight starts. Pre-programming the fight (who you want to duel, from where, with what utility) converts that real-time decision into a pre-committed plan with zero latency.
items.preview: Before every round: decide what fight you want. Not "play aggressive" — a specific angle, specific utility setup, specific route. No plan = random results regardless of mechanics. Ask: "What is the one thing I can do this round that meaningfully improves our win probability?" Write it during buy phase. Execute it.

items.name: Hindsight vs. Technique
items.source: OD26 — "We Cured This Clove's Ranked Anxiety"
items.why: Reviewing your own play and punishing yourself for "bad" decisions is often analytically incorrect. Many deaths come from hindsight bias — you only know it was a mistake because you saw the outcome. Technique mistakes (identifiable in real-time: bad crosshair, wrong movement) are the only actionable review targets. Separating them prevents false improvement signals and wasted drilling.
items.preview: Hindsight = info you only had after the outcome (not actionable — cannot learn from it). Technique = something you could have identified live (actionable — drill this). VOD review: only flag decisions you could have recognized in real time. Ask: "Did I have this information when I made the decision?" If no — hindsight. Let it go.

block4.title: Positioning, Game Sense & Macro
block4.subtitle: The higher-level game: where to be, when to fight, and how to read the map.

items.name: UFOs Positioning Framework
items.source: w0rthyTV — "How RADIANTS POSITION in VALORANT"
items.why: Most positioning decisions are made on instinct ("this feels fine"). UFOs converts instinct into a scored checklist, making positioning errors visible and correctable. Its real power is in VOD review: pausing at each death and scoring the position reveals patterns — e.g., "I always die in 0-escape positions" — that were invisible without the structure.
items.preview: 5 yes/no questions: (U) Utility — can a teammate support you? (F) Flash-dodge — can you dodge a flash? (O) On/Off angle — off is better. (S) Support/Trade — teammate close enough to trade? (E) Escape — cover immediately available? Score 0.5 per yes. Below 2.5/5 = death trap. Use in VOD review: pause on every death and score your position.

items.name: OH SH*T Line
items.source: w0rthyTV — coaching sessions / TikTok
items.why: Every position has a spatial boundary — the OH SH*T Line — past which the nature of the fight changes entirely against you. For an Operator player it's the distance where enemies can sprint past your scope. For any defender it's the point where retreat to cover is no longer possible. Knowing exactly where the line is lets you hold deliberately in front of it, rather than drifting past it mid-round and suddenly being in the wrong fight.
items.preview: Before every hold: mentally define your OH SH*T Line — the point past which you're committed with no escape. For Operators: the distance where close-quarters negates your one-shot advantage. For any position: the moment retreat becomes impossible. Rules: only cross the line with gun out and aim ready. Never drift past it passively mid-rotation. If you're past it, commit fully — don't half-fight.

items.name: Driver's Protocol (Awareness Modes)
items.source: w0rthyTV — "How Radiants Look At The Radar/Minimap"
items.why: Radiant players have scheduled minimap awareness — they know exactly which game states allow a minimap glance without dying. Checking minimap during Active mode (live threat present) costs you a gunfight. Never checking leaves you blind on rotations. The three modes create a clear rule for when each type of information-gathering is safe.
items.preview: Active (100% crosshair): crossing danger line, live threat. Never minimap here. Sentry (50/50): behind cover, peeking in/out. Minimap flicks okay when covered. Passive (0%): safe rotations only. Rubbernecking sequence after shots: Kill feed → Scoreboard → Minimap → Crosshair → back to fight. Practice the sequence until it's automatic.

items.name: Dead Zone Protocol
items.source: w0rthyTV — "How to TRIGGER DISCIPLINE like a RADIANT"
items.why: Dead zones are invisible in real time — standing and watching a hallway feels productive because you're "covering." The question "am I supporting my team's effort right now?" is a direct forcing function that exposes dead zone behavior. For controllers especially, the wait time between fights is prime utility pre-positioning time, and recognizing the dead zone converts wasted time into map control.
items.preview: Dead zone = standing somewhere contributing 0% support to your team. Diagnose it: "Am I supporting my team's effort, working against it, or doing nothing?" Controllers: use wait time to pre-position smokes, prepare utility, and read the minimap for rotates. Duelists: use wait time to find better angles. Nobody should be passive while their team fights.

items.name: Numbers Game Framework
items.source: w0rthyTV — Trigger Discipline analysis
items.why: The decision to shoot or hold fire in a disadvantaged numbers situation is one of the highest-leverage decisions in a round. Taking a bad trade in a 3v5 converts it to a 2v5 in seconds. The Numbers Game framework provides the expected-value calculation in advance — so when the situation occurs, the answer is already known.
items.preview: In a 3v5: one kill barely improves win probability. Three undetected kills could win the round. Calculation: expected value of shooting vs. staying hidden. If outnumbered AND undetected: do NOT shoot unless you can multi-frag without being seen. Trigger discipline is knowing when holding fire is worth more than taking the shot.

items.name: Win Condition Hierarchy
items.source: w0rthyTV — "Why Pros Buy Sheriffs"
items.why: Most sub-Immortal players optimize for kills because kills provide visible feedback. But rounds are won by objectives, not KDA. Understanding that kills are only valuable when they serve the spike or the clock re-orients every decision. A player who dies planting the spike gave more value than one who got three kills and lost the round.
items.preview: Priority: 1. Spike (plant/defuse). 2. Time (burn the clock on attack; play for time to defuse on defense). 3. Kills (only as a tool for #1 and #2). Kills without objective = stat-padding that loses rounds. Re-evaluate every "should I fight?" decision through this lens.

items.name: Economy Management
items.source: w0rthyTV / OD26 — economy coaching
items.why: Most sub-Immortal players make buy decisions in a vacuum — ignoring teammates' credits and the round history. Valorant's economy is a team system: one player force-buying while four save generates an underpowered half-buy fight that you lose anyway and can't reset from. Coordinated economy wins rounds before they start by ensuring your whole team has a proper kit — or everyone resets to buy properly next round.
items.preview: Full buy: rifle + full armor + key utility (≥3900 creds). Half buy: Spectre/Stinger + light armor, limited utility. Save: pistol only — protect your credits to reset. Force buy: everyone spends everything (for round 2 momentum or when a save loss ends the half). Team rule: if 2+ teammates are saving, you save. Never force-buy alone. Call 'save' or 'buy' at the start of buy phase and align with your team.

items.name: Spike Timing & Post-Plant
items.source: w0rthyTV — competitive Valorant fundamentals
items.why: The spike plant timer (45 seconds) and defuse timer (7 seconds, 3.5s per beep) are the most consistently ignored numbers in sub-Immortal play. Attackers who don't know these numbers make desperate pushes that cost rounds. Defenders who don't know them take suicidal retakes at the wrong moment. Knowing the timers converts reactive, panicked decisions into calm, calculated ones.
items.preview: Spike detonates 45 seconds after plant. Defuse takes 7 seconds total (first beep at 3.5s, second beep at 7s). Post-plant for attackers: get into crossfire positions before defenders arrive — do not stand on site after planting. Post-plant for defenders: you need 7 clear seconds on-site. With 15 seconds left, defender must immediately push. With 30+ seconds remaining, a slower crossfire-clearing retake is viable. In a 1v1 post-plant: attacker with cover wins; defender must find them before timer runs out.

items.name: Swing Protocol (OD26)
items.source: OD26 — "Learn How To SWING" / coaching sessions
items.why: Half-swings are the worst of both worlds: you expose yourself without the speed of a committed swing and without the protection of full cover. A committed swing uses peeker's advantage; a half-swing hands it to the defender. Timing swings to teammate pressure works because the enemy cannot track two simultaneous threats equally — the first look at your teammate creates the window for your swing.
items.preview: Wide swing = full commitment — don't half-swing. Time swings to teammate pressure (swing when enemy attention is on your teammate). Never dry-swing when utility is available. After a swing: move immediately — never hold the same spot you swung from. Game sense underpins this: know roughly where enemies are before committing.

items.name: Autopilot Prevention
items.source: OD26 — "This Raze Couldn't Stop Auto Piloting"
items.why: Autopilot means executing muscle-memory patterns without updating them to the current game state. This is cognitively efficient but produces consistently wrong decisions in a dynamic game where conditions change every round. The 5-second buy-phase check breaks autopilot by forcing one moment of deliberate situational awareness per round — cheap cost, high value.
items.preview: Autopilot = running the same routine regardless of what the current game state actually is. Each round differs: enemy positions change, economy changes, team composition changes. Before each round, take 5 seconds during buy phase: "What is the most important thing I can do this specific round?" Not your default play — this round.

items.name: Info → Utility → Commit
items.source: Zasko — "You're WASTING Your Utility"
items.why: Utility used without confirmed information is wasted in two ways: it misses the target and signals your position without payoff. The Info→Util→Commit sequence guarantees every piece of utility is aimed at a confirmed target, which means every utility use has positive expected value. Dry utility usage (no information before throwing) is one of the most common errors coaches identify in sub-Immortal VODs.
items.preview: Formula: Info (slice/teammate call/noise) → Util (pop at confirmed position) → Commit (wide swing for the kill). Never throw utility dry without confirming a target. Install this as a subconscious if-then: pick one combo, drill it in range, drill in custom, apply in TDM, then carry it into ranked. One combo at a time.

items.name: Neutral Space Framework
items.source: OD26 — "This Top 1% Aimer Had No Brain"
items.why: Neutral space — the area between spawn barriers — is where map control is won or lost. Teams that contest it gain information, force enemies to commit resources, and create execute opportunities. Teams that ignore it give the enemy free map control and a predictable rushing distance to site. OD26 identifies ignoring neutral space as one of the most common high-ceiling limiting factors in sub-Immortal players.
items.preview: Contest or gather info on the space between spawn barriers every round — ignoring it gives the enemy free map control. Every player should have a neutral-space habit for their role: check it early, force enemies to react to you rather than vice versa. Ask: "What information can I get from mid/neutral in the first 30 seconds?"

items.name: Numbers Advantage Rule
items.source: OD26 — "This Top 1% Aimer Had No Brain"
items.why: Win probability changes non-linearly with numbers advantage. +1 (e.g., 3v2) in ranked is not dominant — individual mechanical variance makes it nearly 50/50. +2 (e.g., 4v2) raises win probability to ~92%, making the tactical decision simple: group and execute. Understanding this prevents solo plays that statistically lose more than they win.
items.preview: +2 in numbers = ~92% win probability — group and execute as a unit. +1 is unreliable in ranked — individual mechanics create near 50/50 outcomes. In a 3v1: fight together — the enemy only wins by isolating you into three separate 1v1s. Always know your numbers before committing to a fight. Never split when you have a +2 advantage.

items.name: Mada's Multi-Frag Protocol
items.source: Zasko — "Mada Makes Fragging EFFORTLESS"
items.why: The 150–200ms kill-confirmation delay is a hardwired habit from games where kill confirmation triggers an action (looting, objectives). In Valorant, kills require no confirmation — the game continues immediately. Training "assume the kill" repurposes those 150ms as aiming time on the next target. Across all multi-enemy scenarios in a ranked game, this compounds to a significant mechanical advantage.
items.preview: In multi-enemy fights: shoot T1 → assume dead → immediately snap to T2. Do not wait for kill animation or audio confirmation. Practice in custom: fire one shot at T1 dummy, snap to T2 before T1's death animation plays. Build the reflex: fire → move → evaluate, never fire → evaluate → move.

items.name: Idle Rest After Session
items.source: Fundamenty — Motor Learning Research
items.why: Your brain consolidates motor skills during rest, not during practice. The 5-10 minutes immediately after a session is when early memory consolidation begins — jumping straight to social media or other stimulating activity interrupts this window. Sleep is the most powerful consolidation tool: ~20% improvement in motor skill speed has been measured after a full night's sleep compared to an equal waking period.
items.preview: 5-10 minutes of quiet inactivity after gaming — no phone, no immediate task-switching. Let the session settle. Protect your sleep above all training decisions: a night's sleep after a training session produces real, measurable skill improvement overnight. Never sacrifice sleep for extra ranked games. The session plants the seed. Rest and sleep grow it.
